annotations.label: Theorem

anchor.snippet: a theorem is a statement that has been proven

annotations.note: A meta-mathematical definition of 'theorem' itself, not a mathematical object Mathlib formalizes.

annotations.label: Proof of a theorem

anchor.snippet: The proof of a theorem is a logical argument

annotations.note: The informal notion of a proof is not a formalized declaration in Mathlib (proofs are Lean terms, not a library object).

annotations.label: Conjecture

anchor.snippet: A conjecture is a tentative proposition

annotations.note: A meta-mathematical concept with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Gödel's incompleteness theorems

anchor.snippet: Gödel's incompleteness theorems show that every consistent theory

annotations.note: Only arithmetization infrastructure (the Gödel beta function, explicitly noted as 'needed for the proof of the First Incompleteness Theorem') exists; the incompleteness theorems themselves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Euclid: angles of a triangle sum to 180°

anchor.snippet: Euclid proved from a few axioms and postulates

annotations.note: The three interior angles of a triangle sum to π is proved as `angle_add_angle_add_angle_eq_pi`.

annotations.label: Sum of angles in Euclidean geometry

anchor.snippet: Under the axioms and inference rules of Euclidean geometry

annotations.note: The angle-sum result `∠p₁p₂p₃ + ∠p₂p₃p₁ + ∠p₃p₁p₂ = π` holds in any real inner product space.

annotations.label: Goodstein's theorem

anchor.snippet: which can be stated in Peano arithmetic

annotations.note: No Goodstein sequence or theorem appears anywhere in Mathlib/.

annotations.label: Fermat's Last Theorem (deep theorem)

anchor.snippet: Fermat's Last Theorem is a particularly well-known example

annotations.note: The statement `FermatLastTheorem := ∀ n ≥ 3, FermatLastTheoremFor n` is defined exactly (with special cases n=3,4 proved), though the general theorem is not proved in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Indicative conditional form of a theorem

anchor.snippet: many theorems are of the form of an indicative conditional

annotations.note: A meta-level remark about theorem shape (hypothesis ⇒ conclusion), not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Even n implies n/2 is natural

anchor.snippet: is a typical example in which the hypothesis

annotations.note: The content 'n even ⇒ n divisible by 2' is `even_iff_two_dvd : Even a ↔ 2 ∣ a`, stated generally over a monoid/semiring.

annotations.label: Four color theorem and Kepler conjecture

anchor.snippet: The most prominent examples are the four color theorem

annotations.note: Neither the four color theorem nor the Kepler conjecture is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Collatz conjecture and Riemann hypothesis

anchor.snippet: both the Collatz conjecture and the Riemann hypothesis are well-known unsolved problems

annotations.note: The Riemann hypothesis statement is defined as `RiemannHypothesis`, but the Collatz conjecture has no Mathlib declaration and neither is proved.

annotations.label: Mertens conjecture

anchor.snippet: the Mertens conjecture is a statement about natural numbers that is now known to be false

annotations.note: The Mertens conjecture (the only Mathlib 'Mertens' hit is the unrelated Dedekind–Mertens lemma) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Axiom or postulate

anchor.snippet: An axiom or postulate is a fundamental assumption

annotations.note: A meta-mathematical term with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Definition

anchor.snippet: which gives the meaning of a word or a phrase

annotations.note: A meta-mathematical term, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Conjecture

anchor.snippet: A conjecture is an unproved statement

annotations.note: A meta-mathematical term with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Theorem

anchor.snippet: A theorem is a statement that has been proven to be true based on axioms

annotations.note: A meta-mathematical definition of 'theorem', not a Mathlib object.

annotations.label: Proposition

anchor.snippet: A proposition is a theorem of lesser importance

annotations.note: A terminological distinction with no formal Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Lemma

anchor.snippet: a proposition with little applicability outside its use in a particular proof

annotations.note: A terminological category, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Corollary

anchor.snippet: A corollary is a proposition that follows immediately

annotations.note: A terminological category with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Generalization

anchor.snippet: A generalization of a theorem is a theorem with a similar statement

annotations.note: A meta-mathematical relation between theorems, not itself a formalized statement.

annotations.label: Identity

anchor.snippet: An identity is a theorem stating an equality between two expressions

annotations.note: A terminological category for equalities, not a specific formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Rule

anchor.snippet: A rule is a theorem that establishes a useful formula

annotations.note: A terminological category with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Law or principle

anchor.snippet: A law or principle is a theorem with wide applicability

annotations.note: A terminological category, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Formal theory

anchor.snippet: a formal theory is a set of sentences within a formal language

annotations.note: `Theory` is defined exactly as `Set L.Sentence`, a set of sentences of a language.

annotations.label: Sentence

anchor.snippet: A sentence is a well-formed formula with no free variables

annotations.note: `Sentence` is defined as `L.Formula Empty`, i.e. a formula with no free variables.

annotations.label: Theorem (member of a theory)

anchor.snippet: A sentence that is a member of a theory is one of its theorems

annotations.note: Mathlib captures membership/consequence semantically (`models_sentence_of_mem`, `⊨ᵇ`) rather than via a syntactic deductive-closure notion of 'theorem'.

annotations.label: Inconsistent theory has all sentences as theorems

anchor.snippet: A theory that is inconsistent has all sentences as theorems

annotations.note: Explosion holds vacuously in Mathlib's semantics (an unsatisfiable theory has no models so `⊨ᵇ φ` for all φ), but there is no dedicated named lemma stating it.

annotations.label: Metatheorem

anchor.snippet: is called a metatheorem

annotations.note: A meta-level concept with no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Important theorems in mathematical logic

anchor.snippet: Some important theorems in mathematical logic are

annotations.note: Some of these (e.g. the compactness theorem, formalized as `isSatisfiable_iff_isFinitelySatisfiable`) are in Mathlib, but this is a vague list rather than a single statement.

annotations.label: Validity

anchor.snippet: A validity is a formula that is true under any possible interpretation

annotations.note: Truth under an interpretation is `Sentence.Realize`, and validity is expressible as truth in all models (`∅ ⊨ᵇ φ`), but there is no dedicated `Valid` predicate.

annotations.label: Soundness

anchor.snippet: The soundness of a formal system depends on whether or not all of its theorems are also validities

annotations.note: Mathlib's ModelTheory is purely semantic with no syntactic deduction calculus, so no soundness theorem is formalized.

annotations.label: Semantic completeness

anchor.snippet: A formal system is considered semantically complete

annotations.note: Gödel-style completeness of a proof system is absent (Mathlib lacks a derivation calculus); the unrelated `Theory.IsComplete` denotes a theory deciding every sentence, a different notion.
